revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Gokoku-Schrein]][[en:Gokoku shrines]][[es:Gokoku-jinja]][[fr:Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:護国神社]][[simple:Gokoku shrines]][[zh:護國神社]] {{Short description|Japanese shrines for war dead}} [[File:Yasukuni Shrine 201005.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|qid=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}} the historical head shrine of the Gokoku shrines]] [[File:Hiroshima-gokoku-zinzya.jpg|thumb|[[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]], the most famous Gokoku shrine in its own right]] A '''Gokoku Shrine''' ({{lang-ja|護国神社|lit=national defense shrines|Gokokujinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} dedicated to the {{ill|Soul|en|Soul|qid=Q9165|lt=spirit|1=Soul}} of those who died for the {{ill|State (polity)|en|State (polity)|qid=Q7275|lt=nation|1=State (polity)}}. They were renamed from '''{{Nihongo|2=招魂社|3=Shōkonsha}}''' in 1939 ([[:en:Shōwa (1926-1989)|Showa]] 14).<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}}</ref> Before {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, they were under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Home Ministry|en|Home Ministry|qid=Q1142265|lt=Ministry of the Interior|1=Home Ministry}}, but after World War II they are administered by an independent [[religious corporation]] {{Refn|refn=日本大百科全書. "[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AD%B7%E5%9B%BD%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE 護国神社]". ''{{ill|Kotobank|en|Kotobank|qid=Q11302260|lt=Kotobank|1=Kotobank}}''. [[CARTA HOLDINGS|株式会社DIGITALIO]]. 2021st year dd月22日閲覧。|name="日本大百科全書"}}. Designated Gokoku Shrines were built in {{ill|Prefectures of Japan|en|Prefectures of Japan|qid=Q50337|lt=prefectures|1=Prefectures of Japan}} except {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}} and {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|qid=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}.{{Efn|{{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and the Ministry of Home Affairs was abolished, so they were not designated by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine.}} The main deities are {{ill|Killed in action|en|Killed in action|qid=Q210392|lt=war dead|1=Killed in action}} from the prefecture or those who are related to them, as well as self-defense officers, {{ill|Police officer|en|Police officer|qid=Q384593|lt=Police officer|1=Police officer}}s, {{ill|Firefighter|en|Firefighter|qid=Q107711|lt=Firefighter|1=Firefighter}}s, and others killed in the line of duty.
Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|qid=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}. They were originally called Shokonsha but renamed to Gokoku shrines in 1939.<ref name=":0" />
They are considered the Japanese equivalent of the {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|qid=Q6800982|lt=Martyrs' shrines|1=Martyrs' shrines (China)}} of other Asian cultures.
=={{Anchor|History}}History==
[[File:Hiroshige III, Big French circus on the grounds of Shokonsha shrine, 1871.jpg|thumb|200px|Horse racing at {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|qid=Q242803|lt=Tokyo Shokonsha|1=Yasukuni Shrine}} in 1871]]
The {{ill|Chōshū Domain|en|Chōshū Domain|qid=Q876267|lt=Chōshū Domain|1=Chōshū Domain}} and {{ill|Satsuma Domain|en|Satsuma Domain|qid=Q676187|lt=Satsuma Domain|1=Satsuma Domain}} in the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|qid=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu era|1=Bakumatsu}} established a place for the spirits of the martyrs and war dead of national affairs, and held a ceremony to honor them. The {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|qid=Q1134237|lt=Royal court|1=Royal court}} also held a {{ill|Satsuma Domain|en|Satsuma Domain|qid=Q676187|lt=Satsuma Domain|1=Satsuma Domain}} ceremony to honor the spirits of the martyrs and war dead in 1868 June 29 (May 10, Keio 4), in accordance with the [[:en:Dajokanbunsho|Dajokanbunsho]], Article 385<ref>「癸丑以来殉難者ノ霊ヲ京都東山ニ祭祀スル件」（慶応4th year [[May 10 (Lunar calendar)|5月10日]]太政官布告第385條）。{{ill|Water Ox|ja|1=Yin Water Ox|2=ja|癸丑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Water Ox|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}は1853rd year （嘉永6th year ）を指す。</ref> refers to 1853 (Kaei 6).<ref>「伏見戦争以後戦死者ノ霊ヲ京都東山ニ祭祀スル件」（明治元年5月10日太政官布告第386條）</ref> The {{ill|Hokora|en|Hokora|qid=Q1442984|lt=Hokora|1=Hokora}} (Reizan Kansai Shokonsha, later [[Kyoto Ryozen Gokoku Shrine]]). On July 21 of the same year (June 2, {{ill|Keiō|en|Keiō|qid=Q776775|lt=Keiō|1=Keiō}} 4/the first year of Meiji), {{ill|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|en|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|qid=Q668338|lt=Chikahito Arisugawa|1=Prince Arisugawa Taruhito}}, the Grand Governor of the Eastern Expedition, held a ceremony in the hall of {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|qid=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle|1=Edo Castle}} to honor the war dead of the government forces. Similarly, {{ill|Han system|en|Han system|qid=Q841985|lt=domain|1=Han system}}s and other local [[:en:Feudal lords|Feudal lords]] held ceremonies for the war dead of their clansmen or at places where they were related to them. The following year, 1869 (the 2nd year of Meiji), "Tokyo Shokonsha" (later {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|qid=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}) was built on Kudanzaka in Tokyo to enshrine the war dead since the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|qid=Q309184|lt=Boshin Senso|1=Boshin War}}.
With the {{ill|Abolition of the han system|en|Abolition of the han system|qid=Q331905|lt=Abolition of the han system|1=Abolition of the han system}} of 1871 (Meiji 4), private temples built by former feudal lords or the people were placed under the jurisdiction of the new Meiji government, and in 1874 (Meiji 7), it was decided to exempt temples from land tax and to pay for ritual fees and repairs at government expense.<ref>「招魂場敷地ノ免税、祭祀並修繕共支給方ノ件」（明治7th year 3月17日内務省達乙第22號）。</ref> In 1875 (Meiji 8), it was decided to enshrine the spirits of the dead since 1853 (Kaei 6) at the Tokyo Shokonsha.<ref>「癸丑以来殉難死節者ヲ東京招魂社ヘ合祀ニ付履歴書等取調方ニ關スル件」（明治8th year 1月25日内務省達乙第6號）</ref><ref>「癸丑以来殉難死節者ヲ東京招魂社ヘ合祀ニ付姓名取調方ニ關スル件」（明治8th year 1月12日太政官達）、</ref> The shrine names were unified to Shokonsha while the various places of worship remained in place as before.<ref>「各管内ニアル招魂社従前種々ノ社號ヲ廃シ自今一般招魂社ト称セシム」（明治8th year 10月13日内務省達乙第132號）</ref> On June 4, 1879, Tokyo Shokonsha was renamed ''Yasukuni Jinja.'' The name ''Yasukuni'', quoted from the phrase「{{lang|zh-Hant|吾以靖國也}} in the classical-era Chinese text ''{{ill|Zuo Zhuan|en|Zuo Zhuan|qid=Q230230|lt=Zuo Zhuan|1=Zuo Zhuan}}'' (Scroll 6, 23rd Year of Duke Xi), literally means "Pacifying the Nation" and was chosen by the {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}}.<ref>{{cite web |script-title=ja:基礎からわかる靖国神社問題】Ｑ 戦前、戦後 どんな役割？ |url=http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/feature/fe6700/fe_ya_05060901.htm |archive-url=https://archive.today/2006083dd44856/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/feature/fe6700/fe_ya_05060901.htm |archive-date=2006-08-31 |access-date=2007-01-30 |publisher=[[Yomiuri Shimbun]] |language=ja}}</ref> It was listed as a Bekkaku Kanpeisha in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].<ref>「東京招魂社靖國神社ト改稱別格官幣社ニ列セラルルノ件」（明治12nd year 6月4日太政官達無號）</ref>
=== Shokonsha === Gokoku shrines (shokonsha) originated in the [[Meiji Restoration]] when it was observed that the concept of honoring war dead was present in the {{ill|Western world|en|Western world|qid=Q160381|lt=Western world|1=Western world}} but not in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.<ref name="shokonsha-reference:0">{{Cite web |date=2013-08-21 |title=How Japan Honors Its War Dead: The Coexistence of Complementary Systems |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/in-depth/a02402/ |access-date=2022-09-dd |website=nippon.com |language=en}}</ref> This was particularly noteworthy with the {{ill|Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|en|Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|qid=Q703942|lt=1874 Japanese invasion of Taiwan|1=Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)}} in which only 12 people were enshrined in {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|qid=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}.<ref name="shokonsha-reference:0" />
{{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|qid=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}} was formerly called Tokyo Shokonsha and was a part of a general system across {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712|lt=Imperial Japan|1=Empire of Japan}}.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' pp. dd8–134.</ref><ref name="nippon0821a">{{cite web |last=Hiyama |first=Yukio |date=21 August 2013 |title=How Japan Honors Its War Dead: The Coexistence of Complementary Systems |url=http://www.nippon.com/en/in-depth/a02402/ |access-date=26 December 2013 |publisher=Nippon.com}}</ref>
The fundamental principle behind the Shokonsha system is that it is designed to enshrine people as heroes regardless of their status before their deaths.<ref>{{Cite book |last=山本栄一郎 |date=2015 |publisher=実業之日本社 |isbn=9784408455365 |series=じっぴコンパクト |script-title=ja:山口「地理・地名・地図」の謎 |ref={{sfnref|山本|2015}}}}</ref>
The Shokonsha system became much more seriously implemented with the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|en|Satsuma Rebellion|qid=Q714820|lt=Satsuma Rebellion|1=Satsuma Rebellion}} in 1877 from which 6,959 people were enshrined.<ref name="shokonsha-reference:0" />
=== From First Sino-Japanese War to Second Sino-Japanese War === The enshrinement of war dead at Yasukuni was transferred to military control in 1887. As the {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712|lt=Empire of Japan|1=Empire of Japan}} expanded, {{ill|Okinawan people|en|Okinawan people|qid=Q81257619|lt=Okinawans|1=Okinawan people}}, {{ill|Ainu people|en|Ainu people|qid=Q101828|lt=Ainu|1=Ainu people}}, and Koreans were enshrined at Yasukuni alongside ethnic Japanese. Emperor Meiji refused to allow the enshrinement of Taiwanese due to the organized resistance that followed the {{ill|Treaty of Shimonoseki|en|Treaty of Shimonoseki|qid=Q269655|lt=Treaty of Shimonoseki|1=Treaty of Shimonoseki}}, but Taiwanese were later admitted due to the need to conscript them during {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}.<ref name="nippon0821a2">{{cite web |last=Hiyama |first=Yukio |date=21 August 2013 |title=How Japan Honors Its War Dead: The Coexistence of Complementary Systems |url=http://www.nippon.com/en/in-depth/a02402/ |access-date=26 December 2013 |publisher=Nippon.com}}</ref>
In 1901 (34th year of the Meiji era), it was stipulated that the "kansai" (government festival) be attached to the shōkonsha that were eligible for government funding, and shōkonsha that were not eligible for funding were distinguished by the term "privately funded shōkonsha". After the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|en|First Sino-Japanese War|qid=Q178687|lt=First Sino-Japanese War|1=First Sino-Japanese War}} and {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|qid=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War|1=Russo-Japanese War}}, the number of applications for the creation of privately funded shōkonsha (private rite shōkonsha) increased, and the Shrine Bureau of the Ministry of the Interior issued a new regulation in 1907 ({{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji|1=Meiji era}} 40), which read The "Shokonsha Establishment" (February 23, 1907, Secret Letter No. 16, by order of the Director-General of the Home Ministry's Bureau of Shinto Shrines) established the criteria for establishing a shokonsha and restricted its establishment to those who were enshrined at Yasukuni Shrine, thereby discouraging its establishment. However, in 1931 ([[:en:Shōwa (1926-1989)|Showa]] 6), the [[:en:Mukden Incident|Mukden Incident]] occurred, and in 1937 (Showa 12), the {{ill|Second Sino-Japanese War|en|Second Sino-Japanese War|qid=Q170314|lt=Sino-Japanese War|1=Second Sino-Japanese War}}), there was a growing demand in many areas to enshrine the spirits of the war dead in their hometowns.
In the 1930s the [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] system was developed with rising militarism to impose more control over the memorialization of war dead.<ref name="shokonsha-reference:0" />
In 1939 (Showa 14), the "Notice Concerning the Establishment of Shokonsha" (February 3, Showa 14, 1939, No. 30, letter from the Director-General of the [[Bureau of Shrines]]) authorized the establishment of only one shrine in each prefecture, with a few exceptions.
=== Renaming === Gokoku Shrines were established by the {{ill|Promulgation|en|Promulgation|qid=Q446780|lt=Promulgation|1=Promulgation}} of March 15, 1939 and the [[:en:Coming into force|Coming into force]] of April 1 in [[:en:Showa 14|Showa 14]].<ref name="内務省令12">{{cite journal |date=1939-03-14 |script-title=ja:内務省令第12號: 招魂社ヲ護國神社ト改稱 |url={{NDLDC|2960149|/1/2|format=url}} |script-work=ja:官報 |language=ja |publication-place=Tokyo |publisher=日本マイクロ写真 |publication-date=1939-03-15 |volume=3656 |page=510 |doi=10.dd501/2960149 |id={{NDLPID|2960149}}}}</ref> The name "Shokonsha" was changed to Gokoku Shrine because there was a contradiction in the name, since "Shokonsha" refers to a temporary or temporary ritual and "Sha" refers to a permanent facility.<ref>梅田義彦「護国神社制度の創設」『神道史研究』15、1967th year dd月、134-149頁。</ref> The name "Gokoku" was coined from the phrase "I wish to establish a foundation for the protection of the nation"{{Efn|{{nihongo|Go|護}} means protect and {{nihongo|koku|国}} means nation}} in the draft order [[:en:Imperial Rescript|rescript]] of {{ill|November 28|en|November 28|qid=Q3017|lt=December 28, 1872|1=November 28}} (November 28, 1872) and in the January 4, 1882 {{ill|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|en|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|qid=Q3427667|lt=Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|1=Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors}}, "If you devote yourself to the protection of the nation," etc., was adopted because it was the most appropriate way to praise the deeds of the deities and because it was familiar to people who had already used terms such as "heroic spirit of national defense" and the like.<ref name="週報">'''「護国神社制度の確立」『週報』第131號、1939th year 4月19日號、2-8頁。Establishment of the Gokoku Shrine System," Shuho, No. 131, April 19, 1939, pp. 2-8.'''</ref> The total number of Gokoku Shrines is estimated to be 131 as of April 1939 (Showa 14).<ref name="週報" />
The status of the shrine was determined in accordance with Article 1, Paragraph 1 of the "Shinto Shrines under Prefectural Shrines" (No. 22 of the {{ill|Edict|en|Edict|qid=Q1284645|lt=Edict|1=Edict}} of 1894), which was revised at the same time as the introduction of the Gokoku Shrine System.<ref name="勅令59">{{cite journal |date=1939-03-14 |script-title=ja:勅令第59號: 明治二十七年勅令第二十二號府縣社以下神社ノ神職ニ關スル件中改正ノ件 |url={{NDLDC|2960149|/1/1|format=url}} |script-work=ja:官報 |language=ja |publication-place=Tokyo |publisher=日本マイクロ写真 |publication-date=1939-03-15 |volume=3656 |page=509 |doi=10.dd501/2960149 |id={{NDLPID|2960149}}}}</ref> They are divided into designated Gokoku Shrines, which correspond to prefectural shrines designated by the Minister of Home Affairs, and undesignated Gokoku Shrines, which correspond to other village shrines.
=== After WWII === After the end of {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} the system was privatized, but the [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]s and {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|qid=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}} still exist today and can be seen as continuations of the Shokonsha system.
With the acceptance of the {{ill|Potsdam Declaration|en|Potsdam Declaration|qid=Q163244|lt=Potsdam Declaration|1=Potsdam Declaration}} in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the {{ill|en:List of military occupations|en|en:List of military occupations|qid=Q16154911|lt=occupation|1=List of military occupations}}, Gokoku Shrine was considered a {{ill|Militarism|en|Militarism|qid=Q177266|lt=militarist|1=Militarism}} institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the {{ill|Treaty of San Francisco|en|Treaty of San Francisco|qid=Q392541|lt=San Francisco Peace Treaty|1=Treaty of San Francisco}} went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its {{ill|Sovereignty|en|Sovereignty|qid=Q42008|lt=Sovereignty|1=Sovereignty}}, the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrines]].
Although some of the deities of each shrine overlap with those of {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|qid=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}},{{Efn| Yasukuni Shrine also enshrines the war dead as Heroic Spirits and was renamed from Tokyo Shokonsha, but includes {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712|lt=Empire of the Empire of Japan|1=Empire of Japan}}, [[:en:Subject (monarchy)|subjects]], {{ill|Koreans|en|Koreans|qid=Q484464|lt=Koreans|1=Koreans}}, {{ill|Taiwanese people|en|Taiwanese people|qid=Q707133|lt=Taiwanese|1=Taiwanese people}}, etc. The difference is that people from anywhere are eligible to be enshrined. }} the deities of each shrine are not separated from Yasukuni Shrine, and they perform their own rituals by inviting the souls of their own deities.{{Efn|There are some exceptions, such as [[Hida Gokoku Shrine]] in Gifu Prefecture and {{ill|Iki Gokoku Shrine|ja|壱岐神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} in Nagasaki Prefecture.}} Therefore, officially, Gokoku Shrine is "not in a headquarter branch relationship with Yasukuni Shrine. However, Yasukuni Shrine and Gokoku Shrine, which both enshrine the spirits of the dead, are deeply involved and have various exchanges. Zenkoku Gokoku Jinja-kai (formerly Urayasu-kai), organized by 52 major Gokoku Shrines, works in cooperation with Yasukuni Shrine and conducts various activities to honor the spirits of the dead. The Okinawa Gokoku-jinja Shrine also enshrines the dead of the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|qid=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}}, including ordinary residents, schoolchildren in distress, and civilian war dead.<ref>[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、212頁</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-03-08 |title=沖縄県護国神社について｜初詣、ご祈願、地鎮祭は、沖縄県護国神社 |url=https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |access-date=2022-03-08 |website= |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220308190702/https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |archive-date=8 March 2022 |url-status=dead}}</ref> In addition, about 10,000 mobilized students and female volunteer corps members who were victims of the {{ill|Atomic bomb|en|Atomic bomb|qid=Q650051|lt=Atomic bomb|1=Atomic bomb}} are also enshrined as deities at [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]].<ref>[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、154頁</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-01-20 |title=由緒 {{!}} 広島護國神社 |url=https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |access-date=2022-03-08 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210120233005/https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-date=2021-01-20 }}</ref>
After the issuance of the [[Shinto Directive]] by GHQ after World War II, the state no longer had the authority to direct and supervise shrines, and the decree stipulating that the deities of Gokoku Shrine were the deities of Yasukuni Shrine expired.
Perhaps due to the lapse of the law and the suggestion of Hideo Kishimoto {{ill|University of Tokyo|en|University of Tokyo|qid=Q7842|lt=Tokyo Imperial University|1=University of Tokyo}}, then assistant professor of the Faculty of Letters, some Gokoku Shrines began to dedicate other deities than Yasukuni Shrine to be enshrined in the Gokoku Shrines. In total, there are 23 Gokoku Shrines enshrining local greats and Self Defense Force officers who died in the line of duty in Sapporo, Akita, Niigata, Fukushima, Tochigi, Yamanashi, Nagano, Toyama, Ishikawa, Fukui, Matsue, Ehime, Kagawa, Tokushima, Kochi, Yamaguchi, Saga, Oita, Nagasaki, Kumamoto, Miyazaki, Kagoshima and Okinawa.<ref name="島矢">{{Cite journal |last=島矢大嗣 |date=2016-10-25 |title=護國神社における殉職自衛官の相殿奉斎等の詮衡の一考察 |journal=神道宗教 |publisher=[[神道宗教学会]] |issue=244 |pages=120–122}}</ref>
According to "A Consideration of the Enshrinement of Martyred Self-Defense Forces at Gokoku Shrine" by Daishi Shimaya, "In most Gokoku Shrines, when deities other than Yasukuni Shrine are enshrined, they are enshrined in a separate deity body from the main shrine, and are clearly distinguished. This is a clear distinction.<ref name="島矢"/>
In 1960, {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|qid=Q34479|lt=Emperor Showa|1=Hirohito}} and {{ill|Empress Nagako|en|Empress Nagako|qid=Q230697|lt=Empress Nagako|1=Empress Nagako}} bestowed the sacred objects to 52 shrines of the Gokoku Shrines throughout Japan, and since then the gifts have continued every 10 years since 1945.
Gokoku Shrines have traditionally been supported by the {{ill|Japan War-Bereaved Families Association|en|Japan War-Bereaved Families Association|qid=Q3662025|lt=Bereaved Families Association|1=Japan War-Bereaved Families Association}} and the War Alumni Association of war dead individuals, who provided operational and financial support. However, as the number of bereaved families and war veterans with direct knowledge of the deceased has declined, there has been a decrease in support for Gokoku Shrines, which is expected to lead to financial difficulties. To address this issue, some Gokoku Shrines have established a new association to promote reverence and veneration.<ref name=":1">[http://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/gyosei/hirakareta/kansa/hokatsu_kekka.files/h17.pdf 平成17th year 度 包括外部監査の結果報告書]、目黒区、dd1-dd2頁</ref><ref name=":2">「戦没者ほこらひっそり幕 目黒護国神社、管理者なく」、{{ill|Tokyo Shimbun|en|Tokyo Shimbun|qid=Q766032|lt=東京新聞|1=Tokyo Shimbun}}、2008th year 7月8日夕刊</ref>
One example of a Gokoku Shrine that faced challenges is the Meguro Gokoku Shrine in Tokyo's Meguro Ward. The shrine was previously managed by the Meguro Gokoku Shrine Venerable Society, which was established in 1959. However, the staff responsible for the shrine died and the land was sold. An audit found the building had been destroyed, and it was subsequently demolished in May 2008.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />
== Controversy == {{see also|Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine}} After the establishment of the {{ill|Japan Self-Defense Forces|en|Japan Self-Defense Forces|qid=Q275488|lt=Japan Self-Defense Forces|1=Japan Self-Defense Forces}}, the JSDF also began to enshrine at Gokoku Shrine those JSDF officers who had died in the line of duty. The first time, the number of people who were killed in the war was increased to 1,000. However, as before World War II, both enshrinement and application for enshrinement were made without seeking the consent of the bereaved families, so the wives of fallen SDF officers who are {{ill|Christians|en|Christians|qid=Q106039|lt=Christians|1=Christians}} could file claims for cancellation of enshrinement and {{ill|Damages|en|Damages|qid=Q308922|lt=Damages|1=Damages}} on the grounds that their religious {{ill|Personality rights|en|Personality rights|qid=Q1065899|lt=Personality rights|1=Personality rights}} had been violated.<ref>Yamaguchi Jigyosha Gyosei Lawsuit, maximum judgment June 1, 1988, Minshu Vol. 42, No. 5, p. 277 (Yamaguchi Jigyosha Gyosei Lawsuit, maximum judgment June 1, 1988, Minshu Vol. 42, No. 5, p. 277).[https://www.courts.go.jp/app/hanrei_jp/detail2?id=52169 自衛隊らによる合祀手続の取消等請求事件]、[[裁判所]]・裁判例情報</ref>

deeds|1=The common end of myriad good deeds}} * {{ill|Greek hero cult|en|Greek hero cult|qid=Q1878906|lt=Greek hero cult|1=Greek hero cult}}
== Notes == <references group="注"/> {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}} ==Bibliography== * 内務省神社局編『神社法令輯覧』、帝國地方行政學會、1925th year * 内務省神社局「護国神社制度の確立」『週報』第131號、{{ill|Cabinet Intelligence Bureau|en|Cabinet Intelligence Bureau|qid=Q11492826|lt=内閣情報部|1=Cabinet Intelligence Bureau}}、1939th year 4月19日號、2-8頁（週報 第131号 - 国立公文書館デジタルアーカイブ） * {{Cite journal |last=森幸雄 |date=March 2003 |title=護国神社を事例とする、都市のシンボル的施設に対するイメージの変遷 |journal=Sociologica |publisher=[[創価大学]] |volume=27 |issue=第1,2合併号 |pages=25–45}} * {{Cite book |last=全國護國神社會監修 山中浩市著 |title=全国護国神社巡拝ガイドブック |date=2013-dd-01 |publisher=かざひの文庫（販売：太陽出版） |isbn=978-4-88469-791-4 |ref=山中}}
